title: Memories Of Terror At The Olympics
excerpt: In 1972, Frank Shorter had to decide whether to run the Olympic marathon after 11 Israeli athletes were killed.

photo.caption: One of the Black September guerrillas who broke into the Munich Olympic Village, killed two members of the Israeli team and took nine others hostage. Eventually all the hostages were killed after a pitched battle at Munich Airport. (Keystone/Getty Images)

title: Is The Death Penalty Dying A Slow Death?
excerpt: A shortage of drugs used for capital punishment is leading some states to consider bringing back the electric chair or firing squad.

title: How The Real ‘Monuments Men’ Did Their Job
excerpt: The film about a special unit that saved masterpieces during World War II is based on a true story. We visit a new exhibit in Washington.
audio: {"wbur":{"fileName":"hereandnow_0207_real-monuments-men","playCount":28,"wordpressPostTime":"2014-02-07 12:35:32","broadcastDate":"2014-02-07 00:00:00","timeAdded":"Fri, 7 Feb 2014 13:06:30 EST","mp3Length":1961852,"assetTitle":"How The Real ‘Monuments Men’ Did Their Job","audioLength":"04:10","filePath":"2014/02","fileID":722365,"audioStatus":"available","availableFormats":["mp3","mp4","ogg"]},"soundcloud":null,"npr":{"fileName":"20140207_hereandnow_real-monuments-men"}}

photo.caption: This 1945 handout photo provided by the Smithsonian Institution shows the rescuing of Michelangelo’s Madonna and Child in Altaussee, Austria. Photographs, maps and records from the real corps of soldiers known as “Monuments Men” who were tasked with protecting European cultural sites and recovering looted art during World War II are going on display in Washington, many for the first time. (Smithsonian Institution via AP)

title: Maine Court: Public Has No Right To Use Beach
excerpt: The state's highest court ruled that beachfront property owners have exclusive rights to two miles of beach the public has traditionally used.

title: Stolen Stradivarius Possibly Recovered
excerpt: Authorities says three people have been arrested in connection with the theft of a mult-million dollar Stradivarius violin stolen last week.

photo.caption: In this undated photo provided by the Milwaukee Symphony Orchestra, concertmaster Frank Almond plays a 300-year-old Stradivarius violin that was on loan to him during a concert in Milwaukee. (Milwaukee Symphony Orchestra via AP)

title: Behind The Peace Talks With Taliban: Who's Looking For What
excerpt: Pakistani officials have just concluded an initial meeting on peace talks, while the Afghan government has confirmed reports of secret talks.

excerpt: The report says the Vatican "systematically" adopted policies that allowed priests to rape and molest tens of thousands of children.

title: Study Shows Record Number Of Criminal Exonerations
excerpt: The number of exonerations of people wrongfully convicted of a crime in the United States rose to a record 87 last year. A fifth of those people had pleaded guilty.

title: Ukrainian Protests Captured On Film
excerpt: Igor Savychenko is part of a group of Ukrainian filmmakers called Babylon 13 that is documenting the deadly anti-government protests in Ukraine.

title: Recruiting Scandal Rocks Army National Guard
excerpt: Hundreds of soldiers are under criminal investigation for a scamming an Army National Guard program that paid millions in bonuses to soldiers who persuaded friends to sign up.

photo.caption: A National Guard recruiter speaks to a potential soldier at a career fair December 8, 2009 in Denver, Colorado. (John Moore/Getty Images)

title: With Hoffman’s Death, A Look At Heroin Use
excerpt: We speak with a Philadelphia social worker and former heroin addict who says Philip Seymour Hoffman's death brings into focus the fact that overdose deaths are a growing epidemic.

title: Florida Authorities Rethink Human Trafficking
excerpt: Law enforcement officials in South Florida thought sex trafficking was an imported problem. While in some case that is true, authorities discovered they have a home-grown problem too.

photo.caption: Location of callers to the National Human Trafficking Resource Center hotline.(National Human Trafficking Resource Center)

title: U.S. Launches Clemency Effort For Some Drug Offenders
excerpt: Attorney General Eric Holder is encouraging defense lawyers to compile lists of clients incarcerated for low-level, nonviolent drug offences, to petition for executive clemency.

photo.caption: Prisoners reach through the bars at the Oklahoma State Penitentiary in McAlester, Okla., Jan. 18, 2008. Sometimes they use small mirrors to get a glimpse of their neighbors and the correctional officers. (AP)

title: NYC Mayor Bill De Blasio Settles In Stop-And-Frisk Case
excerpt: New York City's newly-elected mayor says the city has settled its 14-year legal battle over a controversial police stop-and-frisk policy that a federal judge called "a policy of indirect racial profiling."
audio: {"wbur":{"fileName":"hereandnow_0131_deblasio-stop-frisk","playCount":7,"wordpressPostTime":"2014-01-31 13:20:17","broadcastDate":"2014-01-31 00:00:00","timeAdded":"Fri, 31 Jan 2014 14:34:28 EST","mp3Length":3927932,"assetTitle":"NYC Mayor Bill De Blasio Settles In Stop-And-Frisk Case","audioLength":"08:21","filePath":"2014/01","fileID":722216,"audioStatus":"available","availableFormats":["mp3","mp4","ogg"]},"soundcloud":null,"npr":{"fileName":"20140131_hereandnow_deblasio-stop-frisk"}}

photo.caption: New York City Police Department Explorers attend a press conference held by Mayor Bill DeBlasio announcing the city will not appeal a judge's ruling that the police tactic 'Stop-and-Frisk' is unconstitutional, which the judge had ruled over last summer, on January 30, 2014 in in the Brownsville neighborhood of the Brooklyn borough of New York City. (Andrew Burton/Getty Images)

title: Ukraine’s President Makes Major Concessions To Protesters
excerpt: As embattled Ukrainian President Victor Yanukovich bends to the demands of protesters, a Ukrainian activist who had been missing for two weeks was found today with serious, bloody injuries.

title: ‘Golden Moment’ Helps Gang Members Turn Their Lives Around
excerpt: An anti-violence program called Healing Hurt People is having success bringing gang members together with counselors at just the right time -- that is, right after they've been shot.
